Question 1. Man's last end

  1. Whether it belongs to man to act for an end?
  2. Whether it is proper to the rational nature to act for an end?

Article 1. Whether it belongs to man to act for an end?

Objection 1. It would seem that man does not act for an end.

Objection 2. Further, this seems false as stated in I-II:65:1.

On the contrary, The Philosopher says that every action is for an end.

I answer that, Human acts proceed from a deliberate will and are for an end.

Reply to Objection 1. The first objection overlooks the order of intention.

The same answer applies to the Second Objection, as regards all actions ordered to the good.

Reply to Objection 2. Another answer is found in II-II:23:1.

Article 2. Whether it is proper to the rational nature to act for an end?

Objection 1. It seems that only rational creatures act for an end.

On the contrary, Nature acts for an end.

I answer that, Every agent acts for an end in some mode.

Reply to Objection 1. Irrational creatures are ordered to an end by another.
